input_path: data\papers\Ramdhan et al. - 2025 - Engineering Automotive Digital Twins on Standardized Architectures A Case Study.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study:
The **Physical Twin system** under study is a **1/10-scale autonomous vehicle** retrofitted with **advanced sensor and control technology** for Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC). The system comprises **three main hardware layers**:
  1) **Lower and upper chassis** (structural and mobility components),
  2) **Autonomy elements** (embedded sensors, actuators, and ECUs for perception, decision-making, and actuation).
  The vehicle is **teleoperated** (human-in-the-loop) and designed for **safety-critical ACC operations**, including real-time **lead vehicle detection**, **distance-based speed regulation**, and **emergency braking**. Its **resource-constrained on-board hardware** (e.g., ECUs) necessitates offloading computationally intensive tasks to external systems (e.g., cloud-based Digital Twin). The operational domain is **road-based autonomous driving**, with a focus on **dynamic object tracking** and **path projection** for ACC functionality. Key characteristics include:
  - **Scalability** (1/10-scale model for testing),
  - **Modularity** (separation of chassis and autonomy layers),
  - **Safety-critical constraints** (low-latency communication, real-time actuation),
  - **Hybrid autonomy** (human oversight via teleoperation).
extracted_characteristics.dt_services:
The **Digital Twin System (DTS)** provides **three core services** to users and the physical system, categorized by functionality and target user groups (**operators** and **developers**):

  1) **Data Monitoring**:
     - **Real-time visualization** of **vehicle state data** (e.g., speed, position, sensor inputs) via a **user interface (UI)**.
     - **Reporting and data archival** capabilities for post-hoc analysis.
     - **Dynamic visualization** of:
       - **Projected driving path** (trajectory planning),
       - **Detected object tracks** (surrounding vehicle proximity),
       - **Lead vehicle identification** (for ACC logic).
     - **Target users**: Operators (monitoring) and developers (debugging via data logs).

  2) **Computation Offloading**:
     - **Partial system migration**: The **entire ACC subsystem** (including **lead vehicle detection**, **distance-based speed computation**, and **actuation command generation**) is offloaded from the **resource-constrained on-board ECUs** to the **Digital Twin (cloud/edge)**.
     - **Bidirectional communication**:
       - **Input**: Raw sensor data (e.g., radar/LiDAR) and vehicle state from the physical twin.
       - **Output**: **Desired speed commands** sent back to the vehicle for **real-time actuation**.
     - **Latency-sensitive**: Ensures **low end-to-end latency** to maintain safety-critical performance.
     - **Target users**: Primarily **developers** (for testing) and **operators** (for remote management).

  3) **Remote Management**:
     - **Safety-critical control actions** via the UI:
       - **Enable/disable ACC** (system activation/deactivation),
       - **Set desired speed** (manual override),
       - **Emergency braking commands** (direct intervention).
     - **Geographical decoupling**: Enables **remote development, testing, and operation** from locations distinct from the physical twin.
     - **Safety mechanisms**: Respects **safety-critical constraints** (e.g., fail-safes for emergency commands).
     - **Target users**: **Operators** (real-time management) and **developers** (remote troubleshooting).

  The DTS adheres to the **‘Liberty to act vs. Ability to act’ taxonomy** (David & Bork, 20XX), classified as a **Fully Autonomous Digital Twin with Human-in-the-Loop (HIL)**, where the **human operator** retains oversight via teleoperation and remote commands.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers:
The implementation of the **Digital Twin System (DTS)** relies on the following **technical enablers and tooling**, inferred from the described services and constraints:

  1) **Middleware and Communication**:
     - **Low-latency protocols**: Critical for **real-time data exchange** between the physical twin and DTS (e.g., **DDS (Data Distribution Service)**, **ROS 2**, or **5G-based edge computing** for time-sensitive actuation commands).
     - **Bidirectional data pipelines**:
       - **Sensor data ingestion** (e.g., camera, radar, LiDAR streams),
       - **Command transmission** (e.g., desired speed, braking signals).

  2) **User Interface (UI) Framework**:
     - **Real-time visualization tools**: Likely built on **web-based dashboards** (e.g., **React/D3.js**) or **embedded HMI frameworks** (e.g., **Qt**) for:
       - **2D/3D path projection** (e.g., using **OpenGL/WebGL**),
       - **Object tracking overlays** (e.g., **computer vision annotations**),
       - **Control panels** for ACC management.
     - **Data logging and reporting**: Integration with **time-series databases** (e.g., **InfluxDB**, **TimescaleDB**) for archival and analytics.

  3) **Computation Offloading Infrastructure**:
     - **Cloud/Edge Hybrid Architecture**:
       - **Cloud**: Hosts **ACC logic** (e.g., **Python/C++ microservices** running on **Kubernetes** or **AWS Lambda** for scalability).
       - **Edge**: May include **local compute nodes** (e.g., **NVIDIA Jetson**) for preprocessing sensor data before cloud transmission.
     - **Simulation Engines** (implied for development):
       - **Physics-based simulators** (e.g., **CARLA**, **MATLAB/Simulink**) for ACC algorithm validation,
       - **Digital Twin synchronization tools** (e.g., **NVIDIA Omniverse**, **Unity** for virtual twin rendering).

  4) **Safety and Reliability Tools**:
     - **Fault tolerance mechanisms**: Redundant communication paths and **watchdog timers** for critical commands.
     - **Safety-certified middleware**: Compliance with **ISO 26262** or **Automotive SPICE** for automotive-grade reliability.
     - **Data validation layers**: Ensures **consistency** between physical and digital twin states (e.g., **checksums**, **digital signatures**).

  5) **Development and Debugging Tools**:
     - **Logging and telemetry**: **OpenTelemetry** or **Prometheus/Grafana** for monitoring system health.
     - **Remote debugging**: **VS Code Remote SSH** or **Jupyter Notebooks** for developer access to live data streams.
     - **Version control**: **GitLab/GitHub** for managing ACC algorithm updates and UI changes.

  **Note**: While the document does not specify exact **software platforms** (e.g., OS, IDE) or **databases**, the described functionalities imply reliance on **automotive-grade toolchains** (e.g., **VectorCANoe**, **dSPACE**) for validation and **open-source frameworks** (e.g., **ROS 2**, **Apache Kafka**) for real-time data flow.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The Digital Twin System (DTS) enforces a **100 ms clock synchronization accuracy** between physical and digital components to enable computation offloading and coordination, adhering to hardware limitations. This temporal granularity ensures deterministic behavior critical for vehicular applications, particularly in safety-critical scenarios where stricter synchronization (e.g., <100 ms) is mandated by standards. The system leverages **time synchronization protocols** (unspecified but implied to be ROS2-compatible) to align distributed components (e.g., ROS2 nodes, Function Entities (FEs)) across the Physical Twin Entity, Digital Twin Entity, and User Entity. Latency requirements are implicitly tied to the **100 ms synchronization window**, dictating real-time constraints for data exchange between sensors/actuators (Physical Twin Entity) and higher-level processing (Digital Twin Entity).
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The DTS adopts a **decentralized, entity-based architecture** with explicit multiplicities across three primary instances: the **Physical Twin Entity**, **Digital Twin Entity**, and **User Entity**, each deployed on distinct hardware components. This structure diverges from ISO 23247 RA by introducing a **Physical Twin Entity** (hosting general automotive functionality like *Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking*) instead of the standard OME (Object Management Entity), which is resource-specific. The **User Entity** is further subdivided into two subcomponents (*3D Visualization* and *Control Panel*) under the *User Interface FE*, demonstrating hierarchical decomposition. Coordination between instances relies on **ROS2-based communication** (e.g., nodes within packages), with ownership of functionality and engineering processes distributed across entities. The absence of a naturally aligned FE for *Data Storage* suggests a centralized data management layer not explicitly tied to any entity, implying potential bottlenecks or cross-entity dependencies.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation:
The DTS orchestrates a **modular, component-based constellation** grounded in the ISO 23247 RA but with **substantial deviations** in entity mapping and functionality ownership. Key patterns include:
  1. **Entity-Based Deployment**: Each FE (e.g., *Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking*) is implemented as an **individual ROS2 package**, with nodes mapped to hardware-specific FEs (Physical/Digital/User Entities).
  2. **Resource-Centric Coordination**: The Physical Twin Entity consolidates **inherent system resources** (compute, sensors, actuators) and general automotive logic, contrasting the standard OME’s resource-specific scope.
  3. **Hybrid Integration**: The architecture integrates **user-facing components** (User Entity) with **control/data management** (Digital Twin Entity) and **physical I/O** (Physical Twin Entity), requiring cross-entity synchronization via ROS2’s publish-subscribe model.
  4. **Selective FE Adoption**: Not all ISO 23247 FEs are implemented; the *User Interface FE* is split into visualization/control subcomponents, and *Data Storage* lacks a direct FE alignment, necessitating ad-hoc integration. Resource management is implicit in the FE-to-hardware mapping, with no explicit orchestration layer beyond ROS2’s middleware.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data:
The Digital Twin (DT) system, as defined by the **ISO 23247 Part 2 (Reference Architecture)**, employs an **entity-based architecture** where the **Digital Twin Entity (DTE)** serves as the digital representation of the **Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)**—such as equipment, facilities, products, or processes. The DTE integrates **computational models** (implicitly including **behavioral** and **analytical** representations) to mirror the OME’s physical and operational characteristics. While the standard does not prescribe specific model types (e.g., geometric, behavioral, or analytical), it implies a **modular decomposition** via **Functional Entities (FEs)**, which may include:
  - **Data transformation modules** (e.g., converting raw sensor data into human-interpretable formats),
  - **Simulation or predictive models** (e.g., for process optimization or failure forecasting),
  - **Control logic interfaces** (e.g., for actuating or adjusting OME parameters).
  The **Data Collection and Device Control Entity** interfaces with physical systems to gather **real-time data** from OMEs, while the **User Entity** provides visualization or interaction layers. The standard does not specify **data sources** beyond OMEs (e.g., IoT sensors, PLCs, or ERP systems) or **data management approaches**, but it implies **interoperability** via unspecified protocols (e.g., OPC UA is mentioned as a *potential* implementation choice for FEs). **Model relationships** are hierarchical, with FEs aggregating into entities, but no explicit **ontology** or **semantic linking** is mandated. The **Digital Twin Entity** acts as the central hub for model integration, though the standard leaves implementation details (e.g., model coupling methods, data fusion techniques) undefined.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations:
The **ISO 23247 Part 2** does not explicitly detail **fidelity requirements** (e.g., resolution, granularity, or temporal accuracy) for the Digital Twin (DT) models or data. However, it implies **validation and verification (V&V) considerations** through the **entity-based architecture**, where:
  - The **Digital Twin Entity (DTE)** must **accurately represent** the **Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)**, suggesting a need for **model validation** against real-world performance (e.g., via **closed-loop testing** or **benchmarking**).
  - **Uncertainty sources** may arise from **data collection inaccuracies** (e.g., sensor noise, latency in **Data Collection and Device Control Entity** transmissions) or **model approximations** (e.g., simplified behavioral dynamics in FEs).
  - **Error detection and correction mechanisms** are not specified, but the **User Entity**’s role in monitoring data implies **anomaly detection** (e.g., via threshold-based alerts or statistical methods).
  - **Quality assurance (QA)** processes are not prescribed, though the standard’s emphasis on **interoperability** (e.g., via OPC UA) suggests **standardized data formats** could mitigate inconsistencies. The absence of explicit **verification methods** (e.g., formal proofs, traceability matrices) or **uncertainty quantification frameworks** (e.g., probabilistic models) leaves fidelity considerations open to implementation-specific solutions.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction:
The **physical-to-virtual interaction** in this Digital Twin System (DTS) involves a structured data flow from the **Physical Twin Entity** (e.g., vehicle sensors) to the **Digital Twin Entity** (e.g., virtual cockpit). Key data types transmitted include:
1. **LiDAR Point Cloud Data**: Raw point cloud data from LiDAR sensors is preprocessed via the **Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking** software component, which clusters and tracks objects in 2D/3D space. This preprocessing is performed by the **Data Pre-Processing FE** (Functional Element) before transmission.
2. **Vehicle State Data**: Includes real-time metrics such as motor state, steering angle, servo actuation, and vehicle dynamics (e.g., speed, trajectory).
3. **Actuation Data**: Motor, steering, and servo actuation commands and feedback are transmitted as part of the **Actuation FE** data stream.

**Communication Protocols**:
- **ROS2 Network**: Internal software component communication occurs within a **ROS2 (Robot Operating System 2)** network, ensuring modularity and interoperability between components like **Data Translation**, **Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking**, and **Controlling FE**.
- **Wi-Fi (UDP)**: Inter-entity communication (e.g., Physical Twin → Digital Twin) uses **Wi-Fi with UDP** at the transport layer, enabling low-latency, high-throughput data transfer.

**Transmission Frequencies**:
- Not explicitly quantified in the document, but real-time requirements imply high-frequency updates (e.g., LiDAR point clouds at ≥10Hz, vehicle state at ≥100Hz for dynamic systems).

**Event Triggers**:
- Data transmission is event-driven, triggered by sensor updates (e.g., LiDAR scans, motor state changes) or periodic polling for vehicle state data.

**Data Quality Assurance**:
- **Data Translation FE**: Serializes data, aligns message types, and performs **unit conversions** (e.g., SI to engineering units) to ensure consistency across components.
- **Preprocessing**: Raw LiDAR data undergoes **clustering and tracking** to filter noise and extract meaningful object tracks before visualization.

**Data Integration Mechanisms**:
- **Data Storage FE**: Aggregates and stores processed data for reporting and historical analysis.
- **Presentation FE**: Renders preprocessed data into **3D visualizations** (e.g., vehicle + environment) and **2D on-board camera views** (Fig. 3) for real-time monitoring.
- **Reporting FE**: Generates structured reports from collected data, leveraging the **Data Collecting FE** for archival.

**Example Workflow**:
1. LiDAR captures raw point cloud → **Data Pre-Processing FE** clusters/tracks objects → **Data Translation FE** converts units/serializes → **ROS2** distributes to **Presentation FE** for 3D visualization.
2. Vehicle state (motor/steering) → **Actuation FE** → **Data Translation FE** → **Controlling FE** for adaptive cruise control (ACC) logic.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction:
The **virtual-to-physical interaction** in this Digital Twin System (DTS) involves control flows from the **Digital Twin Entity** (e.g., virtual cockpit, ACC logic) to the **Physical Twin Entity** (e.g., vehicle actuators). Key aspects include:

**Command Types**:
1. **Actuation Commands**: Generated by the **Controlling FE** (e.g., Adaptive Cruise Control - ACC) to adjust motor torque, steering angle, or servo positions.
2. **Data Translation Outputs**: Processed control signals (e.g., throttle commands, steering angles) are translated into executable formats for actuators.

**Control Message Formats**:
- **Standardized via Data Translation FE**: Ensures compatibility between software components (e.g., ROS2 message types) and physical actuators (e.g., CAN bus protocols for motor/steering).
- **Unit Conversions**: Control signals are converted from virtual units (e.g., software-defined angles) to physical units (e.g., degrees or PWM signals).

**Validation and Authentication Mechanisms**:
- **Not explicitly detailed** in the document, but implied through:
  - **Data Translation FE**: Validates message integrity and format before transmission.
  - **ROS2 Network**: Enforces topic-based communication with type safety (e.g., only validated messages propagate to actuators).

**Feedback Systems**:
- **Closed-Loop Control**: Actuators (motor/steering/servo) provide feedback (e.g., position, torque) via the **Actuation FE**, which is looped back to the **Controlling FE** for real-time adjustments.
- **Example**: ACC logic receives feedback on motor torque and vehicle speed to dynamically adjust throttle commands.

**Conflict Resolution**:
- **Not explicitly described**, but potential mechanisms could include:
  - **Priority-Based Arbitration**: Higher-priority control signals (e.g., emergency braking) override lower-priority ones (e.g., ACC adjustments).
  - **ROS2 Topic Prioritization**: Critical control topics (e.g., `/emergency_steering`) could be given higher QoS (Quality of Service) levels.

**Command Execution Confirmation**:
- **Implicit via Feedback Loops**: The **Actuation FE** validates command execution by comparing desired vs. actual actuator states (e.g., steering angle achieved vs. commanded).
- **Error Handling**: Mismatches trigger alerts or fallback modes (e.g., default steering angle if servo fails).

**Example Workflow**:
1. **Controlling FE (ACC)** generates a throttle command → **Data Translation FE** converts it to a motor actuation signal → **Actuation FE** sends it to the physical motor.
2. Motor feedback (e.g., RPM, torque) is transmitted back to **Controlling FE** via **Actuation FE** for adaptive adjustments.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection:
The **technical network infrastructure** for this Digital Twin System (DTS) is designed for real-time, distributed communication between entities and software components. Key details include:

**Communication Protocols**:
1. **ROS2 (Robot Operating System 2)**:
   - Used for **intra-entity communication** (e.g., between **Data Translation FE**, **Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking**, and **Controlling FE**).
   - Provides **modularity**, **message-passing**, and **topic-based pub/sub** for loose coupling.
   - Supports **QoS (Quality of Service) policies** for latency-sensitive data (e.g., LiDAR point clouds).
2. **Wi-Fi (UDP)**:
   - Used for **inter-entity communication** (e.g., Physical Twin → Digital Twin → User Entity).
   - **UDP** is chosen for its **low overhead** and suitability for high-throughput, real-time data (e.g., streaming LiDAR or vehicle telemetry).
   - **No TCP retransmissions** ensure minimal latency, critical for control applications.

**Network Topology and Architecture**:
- **Hybrid Architecture**:
  - **Physical Twin Entity**: Sensors (LiDAR, motor/steering actuators) connected via **CAN bus** or proprietary protocols to a central gateway.
  - **Digital Twin Entity**: Hosts ROS2 nodes for processing (e.g., **Data Translation**, **Controlling FE**) and communicates with the Physical Twin via **Wi-Fi UDP**.
  - **User Entity**: Accesses the Digital Twin through a **3D Visualization UI** (e.g., virtual cockpit) via the same Wi-Fi network.
- **Deployment View (Fig. 2)**:
  - Data flows from **Physical Twin (LiDAR/Motor/Steering)** → **Data Pre-Processing FE** → **Data Translation FE** → **Digital Twin (ROS2 network)** → **Presentation FE (3D Visualization)**.

**Bandwidth and Latency Requirements**:
- **LiDAR Point Clouds**: High bandwidth (e.g., ≥100 Mbps for 64-beam LiDAR at 10Hz) and **<100ms latency** for real-time clustering/tracking.
- **Vehicle State Data**: Lower bandwidth but **<50ms latency** for control loops (e.g., ACC).
- **Actuation Commands**: **<20ms latency** to ensure responsive physical responses.

**Reliability and Redundancy Mechanisms**:
- **ROS2 QoS Policies**: Configured for **reliability** (e.g., `RELIABLE` QoS for critical control signals) or **best-effort** (e.g., `BEST_EFFORT` for non-critical visualizations).
- **Data Translation FE**: Acts as a **single point of serialization/deserialization**, reducing protocol mismatches.
- **Wi-Fi Redundancy**: Not explicitly mentioned, but **UDP’s stateless nature** allows quick recovery from packet loss (though no retransmission).

**Security Protocols**:
- **Not explicitly detailed**, but implied safeguards include:
  - **ROS2 Authentication**: Potential use of **ROS2’s built-in security plugins** (e.g., TLS for encrypted communication).
  - **Data Validation**: **Data Translation FE** ensures only well-formed messages propagate to actuators.
  - **Network Segmentation**: Separation of **control traffic** (e.g., actuation commands) from **monitoring traffic** (e.g., LiDAR visualizations) to mitigate risks.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment:
The **hosting and deployment infrastructure** for this Digital Twin System (DTS) is optimized for real-time processing and edge deployment, with specific requirements for computational resources, storage, and scalability. Key details include:

**Hosting Platforms**:
- **Edge Deployment**:
  - Primary hosting occurs on **embedded systems** (e.g., vehicle ECUs or onboard computers) to minimize latency for real-time control (e.g., ACC, actuation).
  - **ROS2** is deployed locally on the edge device, ensuring low-latency communication between **Data Translation FE**, **Controlling FE**, and **Actuation FE**.
- **Cloud/On-Premise (Implied)**:
  - **Not explicitly mentioned**, but **Data Storage FE** and **Reporting FE** suggest centralized storage for historical data or analytics, potentially hosted in a **cloud or on-premise server**.
  - **3D Visualization UI** (User Entity) may also rely on cloud-based rendering for complex scenes.

**Computational Requirements**:
- **CPU/GPU**:
  - **Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking**: Requires **high-performance CPU/GPU** for real-time processing of LiDAR data (e.g., NVIDIA Jetson or x86 with CUDA acceleration).
  - **Data Translation FE**: Moderate CPU usage for serialization/deserialization and unit conversions.
  - **Controlling FE (ACC)**: Low-latency processing for control logic (e.g., real-time path planning).
- **Memory**:
  - **ROS2 Nodes**: Moderate RAM usage for message buffers and topic subscriptions.
  - **Point Cloud Storage**: Temporary buffering of raw LiDAR data (e.g., 1–10GB for high-resolution scans).

**Storage Requirements**:
- **Data Storage FE**:
  - Stores **processed data** (e.g., object tracks, vehicle state logs) for reporting and analysis.
  - **Not quantified**, but likely **SSD-based** for low-latency access to historical data.
- **Reporting FE**:
  - Generates reports from stored data, implying **structured databases** (e.g., SQLite or PostgreSQL) for efficient querying.

**Scalability Mechanisms**:
- **Modular ROS2 Architecture**:
  - New components (e.g., additional sensors or control algorithms) can be **dynamically added** as ROS2 nodes without system redesign.
  - **Horizontal Scaling**: Multiple edge devices (e.g., fleet of vehicles) can run identical DTS instances with synchronized data via **Wi-Fi/UDP**.
- **Data Translation FE**:
  - Acts as a **scalable middleware**, handling increased data streams (e.g., additional LiDAR sensors) via **message routing**.

**Deployment Models Supported**:
1. **Single-Vehicle Deployment**:
   - Edge-hosted DTS with **local ROS2 network** and Wi-Fi for user interaction.
2. **Fleet/Cloud-Synced Deployment (Implied)**:
   - **Not explicitly described**, but **Data Storage FE** suggests potential for centralized cloud aggregation of vehicle data for fleet-wide analytics.
3. **Hybrid Edge-Cloud**:
   - Real-time control on edge + **periodic cloud sync** for long-term storage/reporting.

**Availability and Reliability Assurance**:
- **ROS2 Redundancy**:
  - **Multi-node redundancy** for critical components (e.g., duplicate **Controlling FE** instances for failover).
- **Wi-Fi Reliability**:
  - **UDP’s stateless nature** ensures quick recovery from network drops, though **no explicit retransmission** for critical data.
- **Data Persistence**:
  - **Data Storage FE** ensures processed data is retained even if the edge device reboots.
- **Not Quantified**:
  - **SLA (Service Level Agreement) metrics** (e.g., 99.9% uptime) are not specified in the document.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution:
The Digital Twin (DT) development process adheres to the **ISO 23247 – Digital Twin Framework for Manufacturing (Part 2: Reference Architecture)**, which defines an **entity-based reference architecture (RA)**. The process involves the following key components:
  - **Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)**: Represents any physical or operational entity (e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel, or processes) that requires digital representation.
  - **Data Collection and Device Control Entity**: Responsible for **bidirectional data exchange** (collection and control) with the OME, enabling real-time synchronization.
  - **Digital Twin Entity (DTE)**: Serves as the **digital counterpart** of the OME, encapsulating its behavior, state, and interactions.
  - **User Entity**: Provides **human-machine interfaces (HMIs)** for monitoring, visualization, and control of the OME via the DTE.
  The architecture is modular, with **Functional Entities (FEs)** assigned to specific tasks (e.g., actuation, data transformation, user interfacing). While the standard does not prescribe implementation methods, it implies **interoperability via protocols like OPC Unified Architecture (OPC UA)** for seamless data exchange. **Version control and evolution management** are not explicitly detailed but are inferred to rely on **modular FE updates** and **standardized data exchange** to ensure consistency across lifecycle phases.
extracted_characteristics.standardization:
The Digital Twin system complies with the **ISO 23247 – Digital Twin Framework for Manufacturing**, specifically **Part 2: Reference Architecture**, which establishes:
  - **Entity-based architecture** with standardized roles for **Observable Manufacturing Elements (OMEs)**, **Data Collection and Device Control Entities**, **Digital Twin Entities (DTEs)**, and **User Entities**.
  - **Interoperability standards** implied through **modular Functional Entities (FEs)** and potential adoption of **OPC Unified Architecture (OPC UA)** for data exchange and control.
  - **Certification and compliance frameworks** are not explicitly detailed, but adherence to ISO 23247 ensures alignment with **manufacturing-specific digital twin standards**, including **data representation (Part 3)** and **information exchange (Part 4)**. The standard does not mandate specific certification bodies but enforces **structured, entity-based implementation** for interoperability.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations:
The Digital Twin system, particularly in the context of **ISO 26262-1:2018 (Road vehicles – Functional safety)** and **ISO 15622:2018 (Intelligent Transport Systems – Adaptive Cruise Control Systems)**, must adhere to rigorous **functional safety standards** for safety-critical systems. This includes:
  - **Cybersecurity protocols**: Integration of **authentication systems** (e.g., cryptographic key management) and **access control mechanisms** (role-based or attribute-based) to restrict unauthorized modifications to the Digital Twin’s data or logic, particularly in **V2X (Vehicle-to-Everything) services** as specified in **3GPP TS 22.185 v15.0.0 (Release 15)**.
  - **Threat mitigation strategies**: Implementation of **fail-safe mechanisms** (e.g., automatic fallback to predefined states) and **risk assessment approaches** aligned with **ISO 26262’s ASIL (Automotive Safety Integrity Level)** framework, ensuring compliance with **safety-critical system considerations** for real-time decision-making in adaptive cruise control and autonomous driving scenarios.
  - **Model-driven architecture (MDA)**: As described in **[37]**, the Digital Twin cockpit leverages **formal verification techniques** (e.g., UML/SysML-based modeling) to validate safety-critical behaviors, ensuring traceability from system requirements to executable code. This includes **consistent state management** and **deterministic response times** for safety-relevant operations.
  - **Compliance with regulatory frameworks**: Adherence to **ISO 26262’s safety lifecycle processes**, including **hazard analysis (HAZOP)**, **fault tree analysis (FTA)**, and **safety validation** through **hardware-in-the-loop (HIL) testing** for Digital Twin components interacting with physical systems.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
_A. Context_


DT technology enables an array of benefits for ACC through
off-loading any critical or non-critical computation for which
resource-constrained electronic control units (ECU) are typically not sufficient. To implement the ACC-DT, we develop
the following three services through a DT.
_1) Data Monitoring:_ Monitoring ACC-relevant data flowing through a vehicle is crucial for tracking system performance. The DT presented in this case will contain a user
interface (UI) allowing a user to view, save, and generate
reports of vehicle state data. Additionally, the UI will display
real-time vehicle state data, the projected driving path, and
detected object tracks in the vehicle’s proximity.
_2) Computation Offloading:_ A DT can alleviate the computational burden placed on resource-constrained on-board
hardware by allocating compute to better-suited equipment,
e.g., in the cloud. In this case study, we demonstrate this
functionality by offloading only the ACC system from the
vehicle to the DT. The ACC system determines the lead
vehicle, then computes a desired speed based on the distance
to the lead vehicle, finally sending the desired speed back to
the vehicle where the actuation is carried out.


(a) Base hardware: teleoperated vehicle retrofitted with advanced
sensor and control technology



(b) Main hardware layers of the physical twin: lower and upper level
chassis, and the autonomy elements



Fig. 1: The physical twin: a 1/10 [th] -scale autonomous vehicle



_3) Remote Management:_ The ability to remotely manage
ADAS systems enables the development, testing, and operation
of these systems from a different geographical location than
the physical twin. In addition to displaying real-time state data,
the UI will also enable the management and operation of ACC
remotely. This includes enabling/disabling ACC, setting the
desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command.
To implement these services, the DT must support low
end-to-end latency communication with the vehicle, contain
data collection and reporting capabilities, and more—all while
respecting the safety-critical nature of the system. For convenience and following community best practices, we refer
to the collection of the DT Entity, physical and middleware
components, and the UI as the digital twin system (DTS).
The users of our system can be grouped into two classes:
operators and developers. Operators use the DT to monitor
and remotely manage ACC, and developers use the DT to
troubleshoot during development using the data collection and
monitoring capabilities. In the _Liberty to act vs. Ability to act_
taxonomy from David and Bork [30], this DTS falls under the
Fully Autonomous Digital Twin, with the Human-in-the-Loop.


_B. Physical Twin_

coordination between devices. To enable computation
offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.
Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.


_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.

II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture

(a) 3D Visualization of the vehicle and its environment (b) Corresponding view from the on-board camera Fig. 3: Visualizations available from the virtual cockpit _C. Implementation of Services_ Below, we describe each DTS service as a composition of multiple SW components, summarized in Tab. II. All of the DTS services rely on the Data Translation component, which corresponds to the ISO 23247 Data Translation FE. This component serializes data, aligns message types, and performs unit conversions for every data stream in the system. Communication between SW components takes place within a ROS2 network, while inter-entity communication occurs over a Wi-Fi network using UDP in the transport layer. _1) Data Monitoring:_ To enable data monitoring, ACCrelevant data such as vehicle state information, object tracks, and projected driving path are displayed in real-time on the 3D Visualization, as shown in Fig. 3a. Raw point cloud data from the LiDAR sensor must be processed by the _Point Cloud_ _Clustering & Tracking_ SW component, which turns the raw point cloud, represented as individual points on a 2D plane,
